title: Context drift in research agents
text: Context drift appears when an agent keeps appending retrieved snippets, tool logs, and draft conclusions into the same prompt. The model attends to stale or high-salience text even when the task has shifted, so later answers repeat early assumptions instead of following new evidence.

title: Unsupported claims require citation discipline
text: Unsupported claims are reduced when every synthesized claim is attached to a retrieved evidence ID. A citation is not a decoration; it is a contract that the cited source actually supports the claim.

title: Citation verifier as a guardrail
text: A citation verifier catches answers that cite unknown evidence IDs or cite a source whose text does not overlap the claim. It is cheap, deterministic, and useful even when a later LLM judge is available.

title: Noisy retrieval and source diversity
text: Noisy retrieval often comes from shallow lexical top-k search returning many near-duplicates from the same source. Source diversity and query expansion improve recall by trading some precision for broader evidence coverage.

title: Memory degradation
text: Memory degradation happens when an agent stores unfiltered summaries, guesses, and outdated plans. The memory begins to look authoritative even though it no longer represents the best available evidence.

title: Source-grounded note memory
text: Source-grounded memory stores compact notes with evidence IDs and source names. It separates scratchpad reasoning from durable research notes, which makes later synthesis easier to audit.

title: Evaluation mismatch
text: Evaluation mismatch occurs when an answer looks fluent to a human reviewer but fails the intended measurement, such as evidence recall or unsupported claim rate. Agent evaluations must measure the behavior they claim to improve.

title: Config adaptation instead of code rewriting
text: A practical stem agent can adapt typed architecture configuration rather than rewrite arbitrary code. The runtime remains stable while retrieval depth, memory policy, tools, retries, and planning style change under validation.

title: Freezing a specialist architecture
text: Freezing means the agent stops mutating its architecture after validation performance stabilizes. The frozen execution agent is less flexible but easier to reproduce, debug, and evaluate on holdout tasks.

title: Mutation budget and rollback
text: A mutation budget prevents endless architecture search. Invalid candidates should be rejected by schema validation or rolled back after failed validation, not patched silently during a benchmark run.

title: Broken tool calls
text: Broken tool calls include malformed arguments, transient timeouts, empty responses, and hidden partial failures. A reliable agent logs every attempt, distinguishes tool failure from no evidence found, and uses bounded retries.

title: Fluent but wrong can score high with bad rubrics
text: A judge that rewards coherence may overrate an answer that misses required evidence. This contradiction is why citation-level checks and deterministic coverage metrics are valuable in research-agent evaluation.

title: Strict evidence metrics can miss useful synthesis
text: Strict evidence metrics can underrate an answer that correctly integrates sources but uses a different phrasing than the expected rubric. The evaluation should report this limitation instead of pretending the metric is complete.
